front: Work: wagons 'rumble and roll,' horses 'whinny and neigh'; depicts conscripts marching to war
back: Song of the Wagons / Ballad of the Army Carts (Du Fu)

front: Work: 'having girls is best' because 'our sons are merely buried amid the grass'
back: Song of the Wagons / Ballad of the Army Carts (Du Fu)

front: Work: dust stirred up hides a bridge across the Wei river; new and old ghosts weep
back: Song of the Wagons / Ballad of the Army Carts (Du Fu)

front: Work: 'the country is broken/shattered, but mountains and rivers remain'
Work: the speaker's white hair is now too sparse to hold a hairpin; written while held captive during the An Lushan Rebellion
Work: this poem was greatly admired by Matsuo Basho and paraphrased in The Narrow Road to the Deep North
Work: the autumn wind 'angrily howls' and tears the roof off the poet's thatched cottage
back: My Cottage Unroofed by Autumn Gales (Du Fu)

front: Work: a museum in Chengdu honors this poet's thatched cottage
Work: 'Thinking of Li Bai at the End of the Sky' — asks 'What time will the wild goose come?'
Work: 'Dreaming of Li Bai' — asks 'Caught in a net, how is it you still have wings?'; mistakes moonlight for Li Bai's face
Work: a 100-couplet poem themed on an 'Autumn Day'; a later poet borrowed the rhymes after the Crow Terrace Poetry Trial
back: Autumn Day in Kui Prefecture / Autumn Meditations (Du Fu)

front: Author: known as the 'Poet-Sage' or 'Poet-Saint'; forms the 'Great Li-Du' with Li Bai
Author: had the most poems (39) in the standard Three Hundred Tang Poems anthology
Author: master of the lushi (eight-line regulated verse) style
Author: Li Bai's only known poem to this poet asks 'how come you've grown so thin?' — 'you must be suffering from too much poetry'
